Task: Refactor the TP-Tutor dashboard from a "sliding panel" architecture into a "Unified Mosaic Grid" inspired by the Windows Phone Live Tile interface.

Core Requirements:

The Grid Engine:

Create a single, scrollable canvas that supports both vertical and horizontal movement (an "Infinite Surface" feel).

The center (0,0) must be anchored by two primary tiles: a Daily Review tile (using the app's gold/action color) and a Roadmap tile.

Flank the center with "Wings": The Vocab Grid tiles to the left and Phrasebook tiles to the right.

The Achievements/Breakthrough Logs should be positioned in a section below the main Roadmap.

Physics & Boundaries:

No Overlapping: Remove all Z-axis stacking. Tiles must be "citizens" of a single grid layer. No panel should ever slide over another.

Magnetic Snapping: Implement a "snap-to-tile" logic. When the user scrolls, the viewport must magnetically snap to the nearest primary component (Roadmap, Vocab Grid, or Phrasebook) so they are never cut off.

Rubber-Band Resistance: If the user attempts to scroll beyond the defined content, implement a high-resistance "rubber-band" effect that pulls them back.

Tile States & Types:

Live Tiles: Tiles should support different sizes (1x1 squares, 2x1 rectangles).

Grammar Injection: The main Vocab Grid must now support "Grammar Tiles" alongside "Word Tiles." Grammar tiles should be visually distinguished with a distinct border or book icon.

Expansion Logic: Tapping a tile (like the Roadmap) should trigger a "hero expansion" where the tile grows to fill the screen while the surrounding tiles scale down or shift out of view.

Visual Language:

The layout must feel high-tech and interconnected. Use consistent gaps (gutters) between tiles.

The Roadmap tile must show current progress (e.g., "Introduction Textbook: 45% Complete").

Constraint: Do not break the existing Firebase syncing or the masteryStore logic. Use the current mastery levels (⬜ to ✅) to determine the background colors/intensity of the tiles.

svo_intro — SVO Sentence Structure

requiredVocabIds: ["mi", "sina", "ona"]
requiredGrammarIds: []

li_rule — The Divider 'li'

requiredVocabIds: ["li", "ona", "jan", "toki", "pona"]
requiredGrammarIds: ["particle_li"]
What it is: "li" separates subject from verb/predicate when subject is NOT mi or sina alone
Teach: jan Lina contrasts "mi pona" vs "ona li pona" — why no li in the first case
Practice: Given sentences, decide where li goes (or doesn't)

e_rule — The Direct Object 'e'

requiredVocabIds: ["e", "mi", "sina", "ona", "moku", "toki", "pona", "ike"]
requiredGrammarIds: ["particle_e"]
What it is: "e" marks the direct object; without it, the predicate is intransitive
Teach: "mi moku" (I eat) vs "mi moku e kili" (I eat fruit)
Practice: Add e + object to intransitive sentences
Quiz: Identify which slot e belongs in; translate transitive sentences

mi_sina_exception — The mi/sina Exception

requiredVocabIds: ["mi", "sina"]
requiredGrammarIds: ["particle_li"]
What it is: When subject is exactly "mi" or "sina" (alone, unmodified), li is dropped
Teach: jan Lina drills the contrast: "mi pona" ✓ vs "mi mute li pona" ✓

en_conjunction — Connecting Subjects with 'en'

requiredVocabIds: ["en", "mi", "sina", "ona", "jan"]
requiredGrammarIds: ["particle_li"]
What it is: "en" joins multiple subjects; crucially, this always requires li (mi en sina li pona)

This will be long and detailed — I'll cover every node with full part-of-speech breakdowns, real examples, and distinct teach/practice/review activities.

jan Lina Curriculum — Deep Node Reference

Stage 1: The Sound of Simplicity

phi_sim — Philosophy of Simplicity
What it is
Toki Pona was created in 2001 by Canadian linguist Sonja Lang as a thought experiment: can you build a language so small it forces clarity? ~120 words. No exceptions. No conjugation. No tense. The constraint isn't poverty — it's pressure. Like a haiku, the form shapes the thought.
Core ideas to convey:

Simplicity as a deliberate philosophy, not a limitation
Ambiguity is a feature — context does the work
The language changes how you think, not just how you speak

Examples worth discussing:

"pona" means good, simple, fixed, friendly, correct — one word holds all of these because they're all the same idea at the root
Translating "I'm stressed about my career" forces you to ask: what do I actually mean? "mi pilin ike" (I feel bad) is more honest than the English abstraction
There's no word for "war" — you'd say "utala mute" (much conflict) — which makes you reckon with what war actually is

Teach:

jan Lina asks the student: "What's one thing in your life you overcomplicate with words?" Then they work through expressing it simply — still in English, but thinking in Toki Pona constraints
Discuss: what would you lose if you had only 120 words? What might you gain?

Practice:

Take 5 English abstract nouns (anxiety, justice, ambition, nostalgia, power) and find the Toki Pona core concept hiding inside each one
No Toki Pona required yet — this is conceptual

Review:

Student explains back: what is Toki Pona trying to do? Why does it work this way?
No wrong answers — but jan Lina pushes for depth


vowels — Universal Vowels (a e i o u)
What it is
Every vowel has exactly one sound, always. No silent letters. No diphthongs. No "a says 'ay' sometimes." This is Spanish/Italian vowel logic.
LetterSoundAs inaahfathereehbedieemachineoohsolaruooflute
Examples:

"toki" = TOH-kee (not TOH-kai or TOK-ee)
"pona" = POH-nah (not POH-nuh)
"suli" = SOO-lee
"pilin" = PEE-leen
"moku" = MOH-koo

Teach:

jan Lina reads each vowel sound; student echoes and writes the phonetic spelling of 5 words they'll use a lot (their name, their city, etc.)

Practice:

Given 8 written Toki Pona words, student writes phonetic pronunciation
Reverse: given a phonetic pronunciation, student writes the Toki Pona spelling

Review:

jan Lina says (describes) a mispronounced word — student identifies which vowel is wrong and corrects it
E.g. "Someone says 'TOH-kye' — what's wrong and how should it sound?"


consonants — The Nine Consonants
What it is
Only these nine: j, k, l, m, n, p, s, t, w
Gone: b, c, d, f, g, h, q, r, v, x, y, z
Notable sounds:

j sounds like English "y" — "jan" = "yan"
No r — this trips up Romance language speakers most
No h — despite feeling natural in many languages
All others sound close to English equivalents

Examples of the "j = y" trap:

"jan" = "yan" (person) — not "jan" like "January"
"jelo" = "YEH-loh" (yellow) — not "JEH-loh"
"jo" = "yoh" (to have) — not "joe"

Examples of words that use all nine consonants:

"toki pona li pona mute" — t, k, p, l, p, n, l, p, n, m, t — all within two sentences you'll use constantly

Teach:

Consonant by consonant, with special attention to j
Show which English sounds get swapped when adapting names: r→l, f→p, h→drops or k, v→w or p

Practice:

Take 10 English words/names and identify which consonants need to change and what they become
E.g. "Robert" → l replaces r, b drops or becomes p → "Lopeto" or "Lope"

Review:

Given a list including invalid consonants (b, r, f, h mixed in), student identifies and removes the illegals
Student adapts 3 words containing r, f, and v into Toki Pona-legal consonants


syllables — The Syllable Equation
What it is
Formula: (C)V(n)

Optional consonant → required vowel → optional "n"
Every syllable must have exactly one vowel
The "n" can only close a syllable if the next syllable doesn't start with n or m

Forbidden combos (memorize these):

ji — banned
ti — banned (use "si" instead)
wo — banned
wu — banned
nn — can't have n-n across syllable boundary
nm — can't have n-m across syllable boundary

Valid syllable examples:

a, e, i, o, u (just vowels — valid)
pa, ke, li, mo, su (C+V — valid)
pan, ken, lin, mon, sun (C+V+n — valid)
an, en, in, on, un (V+n — valid)

Invalid syllable examples:

"ji" — banned; "si" is the substitution
"ti" — banned; "si" is the substitution
"wo" — banned; "o" alone or restructure
"bla" — b is not a valid consonant anyway, but also: no consonant clusters

Real word breakdowns:

"toki" = TO-ki
"kepeken" = KE-pe-ken
"tenpo" = TEN-po
"sitelen" = SI-te-len
"nanpa" = NAN-pa

Teach:

Walk through the formula with 10 words the student will actually use
Explicitly drill the forbidden combos — they come up in name adaptation

Practice:

Given 12 syllables (mix of valid and invalid), student marks each valid or invalid and explains why
Break 8 Toki Pona words into their syllable components

Review:

Student is given a foreign word and must construct a valid Toki Pona adaptation syllable by syllable
E.g. "Chicago" → "Sikako" (ch→s, i valid, c→k, a valid, g→k, o valid)


stress — The Pulse (Initial Stress)
What it is
Every Toki Pona word stresses the first syllable. Always. No exceptions. This is unlike English where stress moves and carries meaning (REcord vs reCORD).
Why it matters:

Getting stress wrong makes you sound uncertain and can make words harder to parse in conversation
Initial stress also gives Toki Pona its characteristic rhythmic feel — forward-leaning, grounded

Examples:

"TO-ki" not "to-KI"
"PON-a" not "pon-A"
"KE-pe-ken" not "ke-PE-ken" or "ke-pe-KEN"
"SI-te-len" not "si-TE-len"
"TEN-po" not "ten-PO"

Contrast with English traps:

"nanpa" (number) — English speakers want "nan-PA" because it sounds like "numb-er" stress — wrong. "NAN-pa"
"kalama" (sound/noise) — "KA-la-ma" not "ka-LA-ma"

Teach:

jan Lina models each word; student identifies which syllable carries the beat
Clap/tap exercise: one tap per syllable, heavy tap on first

Practice:

10 words written out, student marks the stressed syllable
Student says 5 words out loud (or writes phonetic with caps marking stress)

Review:

jan Lina presents "student errors" — mispronounced words with wrong stress — student corrects them
E.g. "'mi toki' — but someone says it like 'mi to-KI' — what's wrong?"


name_adapt — Name Adaptation
What it is
Foreign proper names must be adapted to Toki Pona phonology. They function as proper adjectives, not standalone nouns — they follow a head noun like "jan" (person), "ma" (land), "tomo" (city/building).
The rules:

Apply only valid consonants (swap r→l, f→p, h→k or drop, v→w or p, etc.)
Apply only valid syllable structures — break up consonant clusters
Apply only valid vowels — approximate the original sound
Capitalize the adapted name (it remains a proper adjective)
Forbidden combos still apply (no ji, ti, wo, wu)

Examples (real people and places):

Michael → Maikolo (mai-ko-lo)
Sarah → Sawa (s-awa, r→w)
Chicago → Sikako
Japan → Nijon (from Japanese "Nippon" → Nijon)
France → Palanse (f→p, r→l)
New York → Nujoka
London → Lanten
Brazil → Pasila (b→p, r→l, z→s)

Head noun usage:

"jan Maikolo" = Michael (the person)
"ma Palanse" = France (the country)
"tomo Nujoka" = New York City
"ma tomo Lanten" = the city of London

Teach:

jan Lina adapts the student's name with them, step by step, explaining each substitution
Then: their hometown, their best friend's name

Practice:

Student adapts 6 names meaningful to them (from their lore — real friends, places)
For each, write the full "jan/ma/tomo + adapted name" form

Review:

Given 5 adapted names, student identifies which ones violate phonology rules and corrects them
E.g. "jan Richa" — r is invalid, should be "jan Lija" or similar
Student writes their own full Toki Pona name + origin + city in one sentence (in English structure is fine — grammar comes later)


cp1 — Checkpoint: The Sound of Simplicity
What it tests:

Vowel sounds (production and recognition)
Consonant validity
Syllable structure (valid vs invalid)
Stress placement
Name adaptation

Format:

5 words to phonetically transcribe
5 syllables to mark valid/invalid
3 words to mark stress on
2 names to adapt into Toki Pona (one person, one place)
Adapt their own full name if not already done

Pass criteria: Student can adapt their own name correctly, pronounce any Toki Pona word given to them, and identify invalid syllables.

Stage 2: Building the Core

svo_intro — SVO Sentence Structure
What it is
Toki Pona sentences follow Subject → (li) → Verb/Predicate → (e) → Object. Like English, unlike Latin or Japanese. The simplicity here is radical: no tense, no conjugation, no agreement. The verb is identical in every context.
Parts of speech note:
Most Toki Pona words can function as noun, verb, or adjective depending on position. "toki" in subject position = speech. "toki" in predicate position = to speak/communicate. Position determines role.
Examples (real, non-trivial):

"mi toki" — I speak / I am communicating
"sina sona" — you know / you understand
"ona li pali" — they work / they are doing something
"mi pilin e ni" — I feel this / I sense this thing
"sina jo e sona" — you have knowledge / you understand it (lit. you hold knowledge)
"ona li lukin e tenpo pini" — they look at the past / they're reflecting on what was

Teach:

Three slots: who → does what → to what
Emphasize: the verb doesn't change for person, tense, or number
"ona li pali" works for "she works," "they worked," "he has been working" — context decides

Practice:

Given 8 English sentences, student identifies the S, V, O components, then maps them to Toki Pona slots (words provided)
Student builds 5 sentences using only mi, sina, ona + a given verb bank

Review:

jan Lina says a Toki Pona sentence; student identifies which slot each word occupies
Student corrects scrambled sentences (words in wrong order)
Translate 4 English sentences that require thinking about what the "real" subject/verb/object is (e.g. "It's raining" — what does that become? "telo li kama" — water comes)


li_rule — The Divider 'li'
What it is
"li" is a grammatical particle that separates the subject from the predicate. Think of it as a verbal equals sign: "subject = what it does or is."
Part of speech: Particle only. "li" has no other function.
The rule:

Required whenever the subject is anything other than "mi" or "sina" alone
That means: ona, jan, any noun, any modified noun phrase, compound subjects with "en"

Examples:

"ona li sona" — they know
"jan li toki" — the person speaks / people speak
"tomo li suli" — the house is big / buildings are large
"tenpo ni li pona" — this moment is good / right now is fine
"ijo ale li awen" — everything persists / all things remain
"pilin mi li ike" — my feelings are bad / I feel awful (lit. my feeling is bad)
"jan pona mi li lon" — my friend exists / my friend is here

Multiple predicates (li repeats):

"ona li toki li pali" — they speak and they work (li introduces each new predicate)

Teach:

The visual: draw a wall between subject and predicate — li IS the wall
Multiple predicate drill: how do you say "she thinks and feels and speaks"?

Practice:

8 sentences where student inserts li in the correct position (or confirms it's not needed)
Build 5 sentences with two predicates using repeated li

Review:

6 broken sentences — some missing li, some with li incorrectly placed — student fixes all
Translate 4 English sentences where the subject is a noun phrase (not mi/sina)
Student writes 3 true statements about their own life using third-person subjects (from lore: their job, their city, their hobby)


e_rule — The Direct Object 'e'
What it is
"e" marks the direct object — the thing the action is done to. Without e, the predicate is intransitive (happening, but not to anything specific). With e, an object follows.
Part of speech: Particle only.
The distinction (intransitive vs transitive):

"mi moku" — I eat (I am eating, generally)
"mi moku e kili" — I eat fruit (specifically, the fruit is receiving the action)
"ona li toki" — they speak
"ona li toki e sona" — they speak knowledge / they share what they know

Examples:

"mi wile e ni" — I want this
"sina lukin e tomo" — you see the building / you're looking at the house
"ona li pana e sona tawa mi" — they give knowledge to me / they teach me
"mi olin e sina" — I love you
"jan li pakala e ilo" — someone broke the tool / a person damaged the device
"mi sona e nimi sina" — I know your name

Multiple objects (e repeats):

"mi jo e tomo e mani e ijo mute" — I have a home, money, and many things

Teach:

e is a pointer — it points at the thing being acted upon
Contrast same verb with/without e and discuss the meaning difference

Practice:

8 intransitive sentences → student adds e + object to make them transitive
Student builds 5 sentences using e where the object is something personal (from lore)

Review:

Given 10 sentences, student identifies: is e needed? Is it placed correctly? Is the object reasonable?
Translate 4 English transitive sentences that require e
Student writes 2 sentences with multiple objects using repeated e


mi_sina_exception — The mi/sina Exception
What it is
When the subject is exactly "mi" (I/me/we) or "sina" (you) — and only those words, unmodified — li is dropped entirely.
Why: Historical/phonological convention baked into the language. It's an exception you memorize, not derive.
The trap — modified mi/sina still need li:

"mi pona" ✓ (I am good — no li)
"mi mute li pona" ✓ (we [plural] are good — li required because "mi mute" is modified)
"mi wawa li toki" ✓ (I, being strong, speak — modified subject needs li)
"sina taso li lon" ✓ (only you exist — "sina taso" is modified)

Examples:

"mi lon" — I am here / I exist
"mi sona ala" — I don't know
"mi pilin e ijo ni" — I feel this thing
"sina pali e seme?" — what are you doing?
"mi mute li toki" — we speak (mi mute = we plural, must have li)
"sina ale li jan pona" — all of you are good people (sina ale = all of you, must have li)

Teach:

The test: is the subject the single bare word "mi" or "sina"? If anything follows them, li comes back
Run through a rapid-fire drill: "mi pona — li or no li?" "mi mute — li or no li?"

Practice:

10 sentences, half with bare mi/sina, half with modified mi/sina — student adds li or confirms it's absent
Student writes 6 self-descriptions: 3 using bare mi (no li), 3 using mi + modifier (with li)

Review:

Spot the error in 8 sentences — some wrongly include li after bare mi/sina, some wrongly omit it after modified subjects
Translate: "We (as a group) are tired" / "Only I understand" / "You all did this"


en_conjunction — Connecting Subjects with 'en'
What it is
"en" joins multiple subjects into a compound subject. Crucially: a compound subject always requires li, even if it contains mi or sina.
Part of speech: Conjunction (subject-side only — en does NOT connect objects or verbs)
The li-lock rule:

"mi en sina li toki" — you and I speak (li required despite mi and sina being present)
"jan en soweli li lon" — the person and the animal are here

Common mistake:

Using en to connect objects ("mi moku e kili en pan" — WRONG)
Object connection uses repeated e: "mi moku e kili e pan" ✓

Examples:

"mi en ona li pali" — they and I work / we're both working on it
"sina en jan pona sina li toki e ijo suli" — you and your friend are discussing something important
"tomo en ma li pona" — the house and the land are good / the property is fine
"tenpo pini en tenpo ni li sama ala" — the past and the present are not the same
"pilin mi en sona mi li ante" — my feelings and my knowledge are different (I feel one way but know another)

Teach:

en = "and" but only on the left side of li, never the right
Draw the sentence in two zones: [subject zone — en lives here] | li | [predicate zone — en forbidden here]

Practice:

6 pairs of subjects → student joins them with en and adds li correctly
6 sentences using "and" in English → student identifies whether en applies or e applies or neither

Review:

Fix 8 broken sentences: wrong placement of en, missing li after en compound, en used for objects
Translate 4 compound-subject sentences from English using lore (e.g. "You and I need to talk about this")


cp2 — Checkpoint: Building the Core
What it tests: li, e, mi/sina exception, en, basic SVO
Format:

Write 2 sentences using li correctly with non-mi/sina subjects
Write 2 sentences using e with transitive verbs
Write 1 sentence with bare mi or sina (no li)
Write 1 sentence with modified mi or sina (li required)
Write 1 compound subject using en
Fix 5 broken sentences (mix of all errors from Stage 2)

Pass criteria: All particles in correct positions, li/e distinction clear, en used only for subjects.

Stage 3: The Art of Description

head_initial — Head-Initial Rule
What it is
In Toki Pona, the most important word comes first — the head. Everything after it modifies and narrows the head. This is the opposite of how English adjectives work ("big house" in English vs "tomo suli" — house [that is] big in Toki Pona).
The head is always the broadest concept. Modifiers slice it narrower.
Why it's not just word-order trivia:
This is a philosophical statement — you declare the category first, then refine it. "jan" = a person. "jan pona" = a person [who is] good. You're always starting from the largest, most human concept and zooming in.
Examples:

"tomo tawa" — vehicle (lit. house of motion — a house that moves)
"ilo toki" — communication device / phone (lit. tool of speech)
"jan sona" — expert / wise person (lit. person of knowledge)
"telo nasa" — alcohol (lit. crazy water)
"jan ike" — enemy / bad person
"tenpo mute" — a long time / often (lit. much time)
"ijo ike" — a problem / something bad
"nasin pona" — a good method / the right way / good philosophy

Teach:

Show 5 compound nouns where the order matters — swap them and explain how the meaning changes
"jan toki" (speaking person) vs "toki jan" (human speech / how humans speak) — different heads, different meanings

Practice:

8 English compound nouns → student identifies the head word and translates into Toki Pona order
Student creates 5 compound concepts from their own life using head-initial structure

Review:

8 phrases in wrong order (English-style adjective-first) → student corrects
jan Lina describes a concept using only physical descriptions and student determines the head word that fits


simple_mods — Simple Modifiers
What it is
Any Toki Pona word can modify any other Toki Pona word. One modifier after the head narrows its meaning.
Parts of speech note:
In English, adjectives modify nouns and adverbs modify verbs. In Toki Pona, this distinction dissolves. Any content word can modify. "pona" can describe a person (jan pona), a feeling (pilin pona), a method (nasin pona), or intensify a verb (mi toki pona = I speak well).
Examples (single modifier):

"pilin pona" — good feeling / happiness / contentment
"pilin ike" — bad feeling / distress / discomfort
"toki pona" — good speech / simple language / Toki Pona itself
"pali suli" — important work / serious effort
"sona lili" — a little knowledge / surface understanding
"tenpo lili" — a short time / briefly / soon
"ijo suli" — something important / a big deal
"nasin ante" — a different approach / another way

Verbs modified (adverbial use):

"mi toki pona" — I speak well / I communicate clearly
"ona li pali ike" — they work badly / they're doing it wrong
"sina lukin suli" — you watch carefully / you observe seriously

Teach:

The modifier always follows and always narrows — drill this with speed rounds
Discuss how words that are "adjectives" in English just are content words in Toki Pona

Practice:

Given a head word and 5 modifier options, student builds 5 meaningful phrases and explains each
Student translates 8 English "adjective + noun" and "verb + adverb" phrases

Review:

jan Lina gives a situation from the student's lore; student must describe it using only head + one modifier phrases
6 phrases given — student explains what each means, including two ambiguous ones where they must give multiple readings


multiple_mods — Chain of Modifiers
What it is
Multiple modifiers can follow a head, each one further narrowing the concept. The chain stacks: modifier 1 narrows the head, modifier 2 narrows head+modifier1, and so on.
The stacking logic:

"jan" = person
"jan pona" = good person
"jan pona suli" = importantly good person / a notably decent person / a great friend
Each modifier describes the whole concept to its left

Examples:

"telo tawa suli" — a large flowing water / a big river (not "big flowing water" as separate traits — the big-ness describes the whole moving-water concept)
"tenpo pini ike" — a bad past time / a difficult former period
"jan sona pona" — a knowledgeable, good person / a wise and trustworthy expert
"ilo pali lili" — a small work tool / a little instrument for doing things
"nasin toki ante" — a different way of speaking / another communication style
"pilin suli pona" — a deeply good feeling / a profound sense of wellbeing

Why order matters within the chain:

"tomo suli pona" — a big house that is good (the goodness describes the big house)
"tomo pona suli" — a house that is importantly good (the size/significance describes the good house)
These are genuinely different — "suli pona" vs "pona suli" shifts emphasis

Teach:

Build chains step by step live, adding one modifier at a time and pausing to discuss how meaning shifts
Use the student's lore to make it personal: describe their home, their work, their relationships

Practice:

Start with a head noun, add modifiers one at a time to 3 different heads, explain each addition
6 English phrases with 2+ adjectives → student builds the correct Toki Pona chain

Review:

Two identical heads with modifiers in different order → student explains what's different
Student builds 3 descriptions of real things in their life using 2-modifier chains


polysemy — The Art of Polysemy
What it is
Every Toki Pona word is a semantic cluster, not a fixed dictionary entry. "pona" doesn't mean "good" — it means the cluster of concepts around goodness, simplicity, fixedness, friendliness, health. Context, tone, and situation determine which face of the word is active.
This is the most philosophically challenging and rewarding aspect of Toki Pona.
Examples of deliberate ambiguity:

"mi wile moku" — I want to eat / I'm hungry / I need food / I crave something
"ona li tawa" — they leave / they move / they go / they are going / they died (in some poetic usage)
"telo" — water / liquid / fluid / rain / any liquid substance
"jan pona" — friend / good person / ally / someone who is kind
"ike" — bad / evil / complex / wrong / harmful / unfortunate
"pilin" — to feel / feeling / intuition / emotion / heart / to touch

The point isn't ambiguity for its own sake:
When you say "mi pilin ike," a Toki Pona speaker doesn't ask "do you mean sad or anxious or in pain?" — they ask "what do you need?" The meaning is clear enough for response, even if it doesn't categorize precisely. This is the language working as designed.
Examples to discuss:

"mi lon" — I am here / I exist / I am present / I am real / I am grounded
"ona li pini" — they finished / they ended / they died / it's over / they stopped
"nasin ni li pona" — this way is good / this method is correct / this path is fine / this philosophy works
"tenpo ale la, mi awen" — through all time, I remain / always, I persist / forever, I stay

Teach:

Pick 3 words and give 4 real-world situations where each word takes a different meaning
Discuss: is this a bug or a feature? (It's a feature — English hides its ambiguities under technical jargon)

Practice:

5 Toki Pona sentences → student gives 2 valid English readings for each and explains which context would push toward which reading
Student writes 3 sentences using words from their life that intentionally carry double meaning

Review:

jan Lina describes a scenario; student must choose which Toki Pona word best captures it, knowing it will carry multiple meanings
Discuss: what is LOST in translation? What is GAINED?


cp3 — Checkpoint: The Art of Description
What it tests: Head-initial rule, modifier chains, polysemy
Format:

Translate 5 English compound noun phrases into Toki Pona order
Build 3 modifier chains (2+ modifiers) describing real things from student's life
Give 2 readings for each of 3 given ambiguous sentences
Fix 4 reversed (English-order) phrases

Pass criteria: Head-initial instinct is consistent; can give meaningful multiple readings; modifier chains are intentionally ordered.

Stage 4: Complex Concepts

pi_intro — Intro to 'pi'
What it is
"pi" regroups modifiers. Without pi, every modifier narrows the previous word. Pi says: "stop — everything that follows pi is a unit that together modifies the head."
The classic contrast:

"jan toki pona" — person [of] speech [that is] good → a well-spoken person
"jan pi toki pona" — person [of] (toki pona) → a Toki Pona speaker

Without pi, "toki" modifies "jan" and "pona" modifies "toki-jan." With pi, "toki pona" is a unit and the whole unit modifies "jan."
Examples:

"tomo telo" — bathroom (lit. water room)
"tomo pi telo suli" — house by the ocean / building at the sea (lit. building of big water)
"jan pi sona mute" — a very knowledgeable person / an expert (lit. person of much knowledge)
"nasin pi pilin pona" — a philosophy of wellbeing / a way of good feeling
"ilo pi pali mute" — a heavy-use tool / a hardworking instrument
"tenpo pi pini ala" — endless time / a time that doesn't end (lit. time of not-ending)
"jan pi tomo tawa" — a driver / a vehicle person

Teach:

Parse the same phrase with and without pi side by side
Use the student's identity: "jan pi [their adapted name]" — a person called X

Practice:

8 English possessive/compound phrases → student decides if pi is needed and builds the phrase
Student creates 5 descriptions of people or places using pi

Review:

8 phrases — half need pi, half don't — student decides and explains
Translate 4 English phrases where the grouping distinction matters: "a teacher of bad children" vs "a bad children's teacher"


pi_grouping — Grouping with 'pi'
What it is
Pi captures everything that follows it as a single modifying unit — until the next pi or the end of the noun phrase.
The scope of pi:

"X pi A B C" → X is modified by the unit [A B C]
"X pi A B pi C D" → X is modified by [A B] and also by [C D]

Examples:

"jan pona pi ma ante" — a friend from a different land / a foreign friend (pi captures "ma ante" = different land)
"sona pi nasin pona" — knowledge of good methods / wisdom about right conduct
"ilo pi kalama musi" — a musical instrument (lit. tool of musical sound)
"tomo pi jan lawa" — the boss's office / a leader's building (pi captures "jan lawa")
"lipu pi sona suli" — an important text / a book of great knowledge
"ma pi telo mute" — a wet land / a swampy place / a water-rich territory

Teach:

Bracket drill: draw brackets showing what pi "owns"
Show how English possessives ("the teacher's method") almost always become pi constructions

Practice:

8 noun phrases — student draws brackets showing what pi captures
Student translates 6 English possessive phrases ("the city's culture," "a philosophy of rest")

Review:

Parse 5 complex pi phrases; for each, student writes the English equivalent
Student builds 4 noun phrases about things from their life using pi-grouping


pi_2word — The 2-Word Rule
What it is
Pi must be followed by at least two words. A single word after pi is invalid — that word should just be a direct modifier.
Why:
Pi's purpose is to group a multi-word unit. A one-word "unit" is just a modifier — it doesn't need pi, and using pi there is a grammatical error.
Examples of the error and fix:

❌ "jan pi pona" → ✓ "jan pona" (good person — pona alone just modifies jan directly)
❌ "tomo pi suli" → ✓ "tomo suli" (big house — suli alone modifies directly)
❌ "ilo pi musi" → ✓ "ilo musi" (fun tool / toy)
✓ "jan pi toki pona" — correct (two words: "toki pona" as a unit)
✓ "tomo pi jan sona" — correct (two words: "jan sona" as a unit)

Teach:

The test: count the words after pi — if it's one, delete pi and attach it directly
Quick-fire: "pi pona — valid or invalid?" "pi toki pona — valid or invalid?"

Practice:

10 phrases — student marks which pi usages are valid and fixes the invalid ones
Student writes 5 pi phrases, then deliberately tries to write 3 invalid ones (to internalize the rule by breaking it intentionally)

Review:

Peer correction format: jan Lina "makes mistakes" — writes 6 sentences with some pi errors — student catches and fixes them
Explain in words: why can't pi take only one word?


pi_stacks — pi Stacks
What it is
A single noun phrase can carry multiple pi groups. Each pi starts a new modifying unit, all attaching to the original head.
The structure:
"HEAD pi [UNIT 1] pi [UNIT 2]"
Both [UNIT 1] and [UNIT 2] describe HEAD independently.
Examples:

"jan pi toki pona pi ma Tosi" — a Toki Pona speaker from Germany (jan = person; [toki pona] = speaks TP; [ma Tosi] = from Germany — both modify jan)
"nasin pi pilin pona pi tenpo ale" — a timeless philosophy of wellbeing
"ilo pi pali suli pi jan sona" — an expert's serious work tool
"lipu pi sona mute pi toki ante" — a multilingual book of great knowledge (lit. document of much knowledge of different speech)
"tomo pi jan mute pi pali ike" — a building for many people doing difficult work / a difficult workplace

Teach:

Parse a 2-pi sentence by stripping each pi group off and identifying what each contributes
Show that the second pi does NOT modify the first pi group — it goes straight to the head

Practice:

Student builds 3 descriptions using 2 pi groups, drawing from their lore (describe themselves: "jan pi [their work/hobby] pi [their origin]")
Parse 5 given 2-pi sentences and write English translations

Review:

jan Lina gives a complex English description of a person/place — student translates using pi stacks
Student writes a "bio" of someone from their life using at least 2 pi groups


cp4 — Checkpoint: Complex Concepts
What it tests: pi intro, grouping, 2-word rule, stacks
Format:

Explain the difference between "jan toki pona" and "jan pi toki pona"
Fix 4 invalid pi phrases (1-word violations)
Parse 3 pi-stack phrases into English
Build 2 pi-stack descriptions from scratch

Pass criteria: pi vs direct modifier distinction is consistent; 2-word rule catches; pi stacks parsed correctly.

Stage 5: Interaction

ala_negation — Negation with 'ala'
What it is
"ala" immediately follows the verb (or predicate adjective) to negate it. This is also where "ala" shifts function: as a noun modifier it means "no/none," as a verb modifier it means "not."
Parts of speech:

Verb modifier (negation): follows predicate — "mi sona ala" (I don't know)
Noun modifier (quantifier): follows noun — "jan ala" (no one), "ijo ala" (nothing)
Number: zero — "nanpa ala" (zero, the number)

Distinguishing the uses:

"mi moku ala" — I don't eat [ala negates the verb]
"moku ala li lon" — no food exists [ala modifies the noun "moku"]
"mi jo e mani ala" — I have no money [ala modifies the object "mani"]

Examples:

"mi sona ala e ni" — I don't know this / I don't understand it
"sina toki ala tawa mi" — you didn't speak to me / you haven't told me
"ona li lon ala" — they're not here / they don't exist in this context
"tenpo ni la, mi wile ala" — right now, I don't want to / at this moment, I'm unwilling
"jan ala li sona e ijo ni" — no one knows this thing
"nasin ala li pona tawa mi" — no path feels right to me / nothing seems like a good way

Teach:

Position discipline: ala goes right after the verb, not at the end of the sentence
Contrast noun-ala vs verb-ala in paired examples

Practice:

Negate 8 given sentences (verb negation)
6 English sentences with "no" or "none" → student chooses noun-ala or verb-ala

Review:

Fix 6 sentences where ala is misplaced
Student writes 4 true negative statements about their own life


yes_no_quest — Yes/No Questions
What it is
Repeat the verb, place "ala" between the two instances. No rising intonation required — the structure itself signals a question.
Formula: Subject + [VERB] + ala + [VERB] ?
Answering:

Yes → repeat the verb alone: "moku" (yes, I eat)
No → repeat the verb + ala: "moku ala" (no, I don't eat)

Examples:

"sina moku ala moku?" — do you eat? / are you eating?
"ona li sona ala sona e ni?" — does she know this?
"mi ken ala ken tawa?" — can I go? / am I allowed to leave?
"tomo ni li pona ala pona?" — is this place good?
"sina wile ala wile toki tawa mi?" — do you want to talk to me?
"tenpo ni la, sina lon ala lon?" — are you present right now? / are you here right now?

Real usage note:
In casual Toki Pona conversation, speakers often shorten to just the verb with rising context — but the formal structure is always V-ala-V.
Teach:

jan Lina asks student 5 yes/no questions in Toki Pona; student answers correctly
Student asks jan Lina 3 yes/no questions

Practice:

8 statements → student converts each to a yes/no question
6 yes/no questions → student writes both the yes-answer and no-answer

Review:

Role-play: 10-exchange conversation using only yes/no questions (about the student's actual life)
Fix 5 malformed yes/no questions (ala in wrong position, verb not repeated)


seme_quest — Information Questions with 'seme'
What it is
"seme" is a question word that occupies the slot where the unknown answer would go. It functions like "what/who/which" but determined entirely by position.
Parts of speech: Content word — can function as noun, modifier, or (rarely) verb-slot placeholder.
seme in different positions:

Subject: "seme li toki?" — what is speaking? / who is talking?
Object: "sina lukin e seme?" — what are you looking at?
Modifier: "jan seme li lon?" — which person is here? / what kind of person exists here?
Predicate: "ona li seme?" — what is she doing? / what is it?

Examples:

"sina tawa seme?" — where are you going? (lit. you go to-what?)
"ona li tan seme?" — why is that? / where is she from? (context determines)
"tenpo seme la, sina kama?" — when are you coming? (lit. at what time, you arrive?)
"nasin seme li pona tawa sina?" — what method works for you? / what approach do you prefer?
"sina jo e seme?" — what do you have? / what's in your possession?
"jan seme li pana e ni tawa sina?" — who gave this to you?

Teach:

seme = a blank that asks "fill me in" — its grammatical role comes from where it sits
Practice placing seme in subject, object, and modifier positions across different sentences

Practice:

8 English wh-questions → student translates, placing seme in the correct slot
Student builds 5 genuine questions they want to ask (using lore — things they actually want to discuss with jan Lina)

Review:

6 seme questions → student identifies whether seme is acting as subject/object/modifier
Jan Lina answers 5 seme questions with real Toki Pona answers; student must understand the responses


anu_seme — Choice Questions with 'anu seme'
What it is
Appending "anu seme" to a statement creates a soft, conversational question — roughly "or what?" or "right?" or "is it?" It invites confirmation or offers an alternative without demanding a binary answer.
Part of speech: "anu" = conjunction (or). "seme" = question word. Together as a fixed phrase at the end of a statement.
The tonal difference:

"sina moku ala moku?" — Are you eating? (direct, formal)
"sina moku, anu seme?" — You're eating, right? / You eating, or...? (casual, softer)

Examples:

"sina wile tawa, anu seme?" — you want to go, right? / are you planning to leave?
"ona li jan pona sina, anu seme?" — that's your friend, right?
"tenpo pini la, sina pilin ike, anu seme?" — you were feeling bad earlier, weren't you?
"nasin ni li pona, anu seme?" — this way is good, right? / does this approach seem right to you?
"sina sona e ijo ni, anu seme?" — you know this, right? / I assume you're familiar?
"toki mi li ike, anu seme?" — my speaking is bad, isn't it? / I'm not making sense, am I?

Teach:

Contrast the three question types: V-ala-V (yes/no formal), seme (information), anu seme (confirmation/soft)
Situational: when does "anu seme" feel natural vs forced?

Practice:

6 statements → student adds anu seme to make them conversational questions
Role-play: student leads a 5-exchange casual conversation using anu seme naturally

Review:

Given 12 situations, student chooses the right question form: V-ala-V, seme, or anu seme
Student writes 4 genuine questions to jan Lina using anu seme (from real curiosity/lore)


cp5 — Checkpoint: Interaction
Format:

Write one of each question type (V-ala-V, seme, anu seme) about the same topic
Negate 3 sentences correctly
Answer jan Lina's 4 questions in correct Toki Pona form
5-minute free conversation with jan Lina including at least one of each question type

Pass criteria: All three question forms used correctly and naturally; negation consistent; can conduct basic back-and-forth.

Stage 6: Action & Location

preverb_wile — Desire: wile
What it is
"wile" precedes the main verb to express desire, want, or need. It modifies the entire predicate that follows. It can also stand alone as a noun/verb meaning "desire" or "need."
Parts of speech:

Preverb: "mi wile moku" (I want to eat)
Verb: "wile mi li suli" (my desire is great / I want a lot)
Noun: "wile ike" (a harmful desire / a bad need)

Examples:

"mi wile sona" — I want to understand / I need to know
"sina wile ala wile toki?" — do you want to talk?
"ona li wile lape" — they want to sleep / they need rest
"mi wile e pona" — I want goodness / I need things to be okay
"wile mi li lon ala" — I have no desire / I'm not driven by want (lit. my wanting doesn't exist)
"sina wile pali e seme?" — what do you want to work on?
"jan ale li wile kama sona" — everyone wants to learn (kama sona = to learn)

Teach:

Position: wile goes between li and the main verb — "[subject] li wile [verb]"
How wile changes register: necessity vs desire is often context-determined

Practice:

Student expresses 6 genuine personal desires using wile (from lore)
Convert 6 statements into wile-desires: "mi moku" → "mi wile moku"

Review:

Translate 5 sentences with "want to / need to / must" — student chooses wile consistently
Student writes a "wish list" paragraph in Toki Pona using wile throughout


preverb_ken — Ability: ken
What it is
"ken" precedes the main verb to express ability, permission, or possibility. Can also function independently.
Parts of speech:

Preverb: "sina ken toki" (you can speak / you're able to)
Verb: "ni li ken" (this is possible)
Noun: "ken mi" (my ability / my potential)

The wile vs ken distinction:

"mi wile tawa" — I want to go
"mi ken tawa" — I can go / I'm allowed to go
"mi wile ken tawa" — I want to be able to go (stacked preverbs — valid)

Examples:

"sina ken ala ken pali e ni?" — are you able to do this?
"mi ken ala sona e ni" — I can't know this / it's impossible for me to understand
"ken la, ona li kama" — possibly, they will come / maybe they'll show up
"jan ale li ken toki e pilin ona" — everyone can express their feelings
"ni li ken pona" — this could be good / this has the potential to be fine
"mi ken ala awen lon ni" — I can't stay here

Teach:

Stack wile + ken in order and discuss the layered meaning
ken at the start of a sentence ("ken la") means "possibly" — introduce this usage

Practice:

6 ability/permission sentences from English → student uses ken correctly
Student writes 4 real statements about things they can and can't do

Review:

Distinguish wile from ken in 8 English sentences — student chooses the right preverb
Translate a negotiation scenario (can you do X? / I can't, but I want to) using wile and ken


preverb_kama — Becoming: kama
What it is
"kama" as a preverb means "to come to [verb]" or "to become [state]" — it signals a change of state or the beginning of an action.
Parts of speech:

Preverb: "kama sona" (to learn — lit. come to know)
Verb: "ona li kama" (they arrive / they come)
Noun: "kama pona" (welcome — lit. arriving-good)

Key compound: kama sona
This is the idiomatic word for learning/studying. It's deeply relevant to this entire app — the student is doing kama sona right now.
Examples:

"mi kama sona e toki pona" — I am learning Toki Pona
"ona li kama jo e sona" — they came to have knowledge / they became informed
"sina kama pona!" — welcome! (greeting)
"tenpo kama" — the future (lit. time that is coming)
"mi kama pilin pona" — I came to feel good / I started feeling better
"kama jo" — to acquire / to receive / to come to have
"mi wile kama sona e nasin pona" — I want to learn good methods / I want to understand the right approach

Teach:

kama describes a transition — it's about becoming, not being
Contrast "ona li sona" (they know) with "ona li kama sona" (they are learning / they came to know)

Practice:

6 English "become / start to / end up" sentences → student uses kama correctly
Student describes their own learning journey using kama sona

Review:

Distinguish kama (arriving), kama + adj (becoming), kama sona (learning) in 8 sentences
Student writes 3 sentences about things they want to become or are becoming (from lore)


prep_lon — Locality: lon
What it is
"lon" functions as a preposition (at/in/on), a verb (to exist/to be present/to be true), and occasionally a content word.
Parts of speech:

Preposition: "mi lon tomo" (I am at home)
Verb: "ni li lon" (this is true / this exists)
Predicate (with subject): "ona li lon" (they are here / they exist)

Examples:

"mi lon ma tomo" — I am in the city
"sona ni li lon ala" — this knowledge doesn't exist / this isn't true
"mi pali lon tomo mi" — I work at home
"lon tenpo ni" — right now / at this moment
"ona li lon poka mi" — they are by my side / they're here with me
"pilin mi li lon insa mi" — my feeling is inside me / I feel it internally
"lon seme la, sina moku?" — where do you eat? (lit. being-at what, you eat?)
"ijo ale li lon" — everything exists / all things are real

Teach:

Three faces: location (at/in/on), existence (to be real), truth (it is the case)
"lon" alone as a complete thought: "lon." = "It's true." / "That's real."

Practice:

8 English sentences with "at/in/on" → student uses lon correctly
Student describes their daily environment using lon (from lore — their home, workspace, city)

Review:

Identify which use of lon (location/existence/truth) is in play in 8 sentences
Translate 4 English sentences where "to exist" or "to be true" is the intended meaning


prep_tawa — Motion: tawa
What it is
"tawa" signals direction (to/toward) as a preposition, movement as a verb, and perspective/opinion as a relational marker.
Parts of speech:

Preposition: "mi tawa tomo" (I go home)
Verb: "ona li tawa" (they move / they leave)
Relational marker: "pona tawa mi" (good to me / I like it)

The opinion construction:
"[quality] tawa [person]" = "[quality] to [person]" = "[person] finds it [quality]"
This is the primary way to express preferences, opinions, and subjective judgments.
Examples:

"mi tawa ma tomo" — I'm going to the city / I'm heading downtown
"toki pona li pona tawa mi" — Toki Pona is good to me / I like Toki Pona
"ike tawa mi" — bad to me / I don't like it / that's wrong from my perspective
"ona li tawa tomo pali" — they went to work / they headed to the workplace
"mi pana e sona tawa sina" — I give knowledge to you / I'm teaching you
"nasin ni li seme tawa sina?" — what do you think of this method? / how does this approach seem to you?
"tawa mi la, ni li suli" — to me, this is important / from my perspective, it matters

Teach:

The opinion construction is essential for real conversation — this is how you say "I like" and "I think"
Practice: every preference the student expresses in English gets translated using "tawa mi"

Practice:

6 motion sentences → student builds with tawa + destination
6 opinion/preference sentences → student uses [quality] tawa [person]

Review:

Distinguish directional tawa from opinion tawa in 8 sentences
Student expresses 5 genuine opinions about things in their life using tawa mi


prep_tan — Origin: tan
What it is
"tan" marks origin (from) or cause (because of).
Parts of speech:

Preposition: "mi tan ma Mewika" (I am from America)
Causal: "mi pilin ike tan ni" (I feel bad because of this)
Verb: "tan seme?" (from what? / why? — a common shorthand question)

Examples:

"mi tan ma tomo Sikako" — I'm from the city of Chicago
"ona li kama tan tomo pali" — they came from work / they're coming from the office
"mi sona e ni tan jan pona mi" — I know this because of my friend / my friend told me
"tan seme la, sina pilin ike?" — why do you feel bad? / what's causing that?
"ona li tawa tan mi" — they left because of me / they moved away from me
"pilin ike mi li kama tan tenpo pini" — my bad feelings come from the past

Teach:

Two uses: source (from a place/person) and reason (because of a thing)
"tan seme?" as a shorthand for "why?" — conversationally essential

Practice:

6 "from [place]" sentences → student builds with tan
4 "because of [reason]" sentences → student builds causal tan

Review:

Distinguish locational tan from causal tan in 8 sentences
Student writes 3 real statements: where they're from, why they're learning Toki Pona, what causes a feeling they have


prep_kepeken — Utility: kepeken
What it is
"kepeken" marks the instrument or means used to perform an action (using/with/by means of).
Parts of speech:

Preposition: "mi toki kepeken ilo" (I speak using a device)
Verb: "mi kepeken e ilo ni" (I use this tool)

The verb vs preposition distinction:

Preposition (no e): "mi pali kepeken ilo lili" (I work with a small tool)
Verb (with e): "mi kepeken e ilo lili" (I use the small tool) — "kepeken e" = to use [something]

Examples:

"mi toki kepeken toki pona" — I speak using Toki Pona / I communicate in Toki Pona
"ona li pali kepeken nasin ante" — they work using a different method
"mi kama sona kepeken lipu ni" — I learn using this book / this text is my learning resource
"sina ken toki kepeken nimi seme?" — what words can you use to express that?
"mi kepeken e ilo sona mi" — I'm using my knowledge tool / I use my phone
"nasin pona li kama kepeken pali mute" — a good way comes through much work / skill develops through effort

Teach:

kepeken as preposition (no e) vs verb (with e) — drill the distinction
Common usage: "kepeken toki [language]" = speaking in [language]

Practice:

6 "using/with" sentences → student builds with kepeken as preposition
4 "to use [object]" sentences → student uses kepeken as verb with e

Review:

Distinguish kepeken-preposition from kepeken-verb in 8 sentences
Student writes 4 sentences about tools/methods they use in daily life


cp6 — Checkpoint: Action & Location
Format:

Use wile, ken, and kama each in one sentence
Describe where you are (lon), where you're going (tawa), where you came from (tan), using what method (kepeken)
Express 3 genuine opinions using the tawa mi construction
Free paragraph: describe your morning using preverbs and prepositions


Stage 7: Specifics

colors_primary — Primary Colors
What it is
Toki Pona has 5 color words. Three primary colors cover the spectrum broadly, accepting more ambiguity than English.
WordCore meaningCoverslojeredred, orange, warm toneslasoblue/greenblue, green, teal, cyanjeloyellowyellow, goldwalowhitewhite, light, palepimejablackblack, dark, shadow
The laso question:
"laso" covering both blue and green is the most frequent stumbling block. In practice, "laso sewi" (sky-laso = blue) and "laso kasi" (plant-laso = green) distinguish if needed.
Examples:

"telo li laso" — water is blue/green
"suno li jelo" — the sun is yellow / sunlight is golden
"laso kasi li pona tawa mi" — I like the green of plants / plant-green is good to me
"len mi li loje" — my clothing is red
"tenpo pimeja la, suno li lon ala" — at night, there is no sun
"oko mi li laso" — my eyes are blue/green
"kasi li laso, pan li jelo" — plants are green, grain is golden

Teach:

Use the student's physical environment and appearance (from lore) to make colors personal
laso ambiguity: when does it matter to distinguish, and how?

Practice:

8 objects → student assigns the correct Toki Pona color
Student describes 5 things from their life using color words

Review:

Color comprehension: jan Lina describes something using color; student identifies the object
Student writes 4 sentences where the color is doing real descriptive work, not just labeling


light_dark — Light & Dark
What it is
walo and pimeja extend beyond color into light levels, and combine with "tenpo" to express time of day.
Time of day compounds:

"tenpo suno" — daytime (sun time)
"tenpo pimeja" — night (dark time)
"tenpo suno open" — morning (opening day-time)
"tenpo suno pini" — evening (ending day-time)
"tenpo mun" — nighttime / moonlit time

Examples:

"tenpo pimeja la, mi lape" — at night, I sleep
"tenpo suno ni la, mi pali mute" — today I work a lot
"ma li pimeja tan kon" — the land is dark because of the sky/atmosphere (like pollution or clouds)
"walo li lon sewi" — there is brightness above / the sky is pale
"mi pilin pona lon tenpo suno open" — I feel good in the morning
"pimeja li kama" — darkness comes / night is falling
"tenpo pimeja pini la, mi pilin ike" — last night, I felt bad

Teach:

Pair with the student's actual timezone and schedule (from lore)
Show walo/pimeja as both color descriptors and light-level descriptors

Practice:

Student describes their daily schedule using tenpo + light/dark compounds
6 time-of-day phrases → student builds correctly

Review:

jan Lina describes a situation with light conditions; student identifies which words apply
Student writes 3 sentences about what they do at different times of day


numbers_base — Numbers: wan & tu
What it is
Toki Pona counts additively using a small set of number words. Numbers function as modifiers (after the noun).
Number words:

ala = 0
wan = 1
tu = 2
luka = 5 (hand)
mute = many (20 in formal counting)
ale = all / 100 in formal counting

Basic stacking:

tu wan = 3 (two + one)
tu tu = 4 (two + two)
luka = 5
luka wan = 6
luka tu = 7
luka tu wan = 8
luka tu tu = 9
luka luka = 10

Examples:

"mi jo e soweli tu" — I have two pets
"jan luka li kama" — five people are coming
"mi pali e ijo tu wan" — I made three things
"tenpo sike wan" — one year (lit. one circle-time)
"mi jo e mani luka tu wan" — I have seven coins/units of money
"jan tu li toki" — two people are talking

Teach:

Numbers come AFTER the noun they quantify — "soweli tu" not "tu soweli"
Note: Toki Pona speakers often avoid large exact numbers by using "mute" (many) — precision isn't the point

Practice:

Student counts objects in their environment using Toki Pona number phrases
Express 6 quantities (given in English) using stacked number words

Review:

Given a Toki Pona number expression, student writes the English numeral
Given an English numeral 1-10, student writes the Toki Pona expression


quantities — Quantities: mute & ale
What it is
mute and ale are both quantifiers and intensifiers, depending on position.
mute:

After noun: "many" — "jan mute" (many people)
After verb/adjective: "very" / intensifier — "pona mute" (very good), "mi sona mute" (I know well/a lot)

ale/ali:

After noun: "all/every" — "jan ale" (everyone, all people)
Philosophical use: "everything / the universe" — "ale li pona" (everything is good / all is well)

Examples:

"jan mute li kama sona" — many people are learning
"mi pali mute" — I work a lot / I work very hard
"ale li lon" — everything exists / all is real
"mi sona ala e ijo ale" — I don't know everything
"tenpo mute la, mi pilin ike" — many times, I felt bad / often I feel bad
"pona mute tawa sina" — many thanks to you / very good toward you (a blessing/thanks)
"mi wile e ijo ale" — I want everything
"jan ale li ken pona" — everyone can be good

Teach:

mute in intensifier position (post-verb/adj) vs quantifier position (post-noun)
ale as philosophical "everything" — deeply connected to the phi_sim worldview

Practice:

8 phrases → student identifies mute/ale as quantifier or intensifier
Student writes 4 genuine statements using mute as an intensifier

Review:

Translate 6 English sentences with "many," "all," "very," "a lot" — student chooses correctly
Student writes 2 ale sentences reflecting a genuine opinion or observation about the world


zero_ala — Zero: ala
What it is
"ala" as a number/quantity word means zero or none — distinct from its use as a negation marker.
The three uses of ala — full review:

Negation (post-verb): "mi moku ala" = I don't eat
Quantity (post-noun): "jan ala" = no one / zero people
Number: "nanpa ala" = the number zero

Distinguishing contexts:

"moku ala" (no food) — noun + ala = quantity zero
"mi moku ala" (I don't eat) — verb + ala = negation
"nanpa ala" (zero) — number context

Examples:

"tenpo ala la, mi lape" — I never sleep (at no time do I sleep) — [unusual: in no time, I sleep — actually means emphasis]
"jan ala li sona e ni" — no one knows this
"mi jo e mani ala" — I have no money / I have zero money
"ijo ala li ken ante e tenpo pini" — nothing can change the past
"nasin ala li wan" — no path is singular / there is no one right way
"mi pilin e ijo ala" — I feel nothing / I sense nothing

Teach:

Quick-fire position test: where is ala? After a verb = negation. After a noun = zero.
"jan ala" vs "mi toki ala" — ask student what the difference is in each

Practice:

10 sentences with ala → student identifies: negation, quantity, or number
Student writes 3 sentences intentionally using ala as a quantity (not negation)

Review:

8 ambiguous sentences → student determines which ala is in play and how meaning changes
Translate 4 English sentences using "zero," "none," "no one," "nothing"


cp7 — Checkpoint: Specifics
Format:

Describe 3 colored objects in detail
Write the time of day for morning, midday, night using tenpo compounds
Express the quantities 3, 5, 7 in Toki Pona
Write sentences using mute as intensifier AND as quantifier (one each)
Use ala correctly as negation in one sentence and as zero-quantity in another


Stage 8: Final Mastery

think_tp — Thinking in Toki Pona
What it is
This stage isn't about new grammar — it's about cognitive shift. The student should begin formulating thoughts in Toki Pona before translating them, rather than translating from English.
Core exercises:
Forced simplification:
Take a complex concept from the student's professional or personal life and express it without looking up words — only use what they know. The effort is the exercise.

"bureaucracy" → "nasin pi jan lawa mute" (system of many leaders/rules)
"grief" → "pilin ike pi jan pini" (the bad feeling of a person who is gone)
"ambition" → "wile suli pi pali lon tenpo kama" (great desire to do things in the future)

Inner monologue practice:
Student describes their morning, a decision they made, or something they're worried about — entirely in Toki Pona, without English scaffolding.
Real conversation topics (lore-driven):

Their work: what do they do, why, is it pona?
A recent decision: what did they wile, what did they ken, what did they choose?
Their relationship with simplicity: has learning Toki Pona changed how they think?

Examples of complex thought in Toki Pona:

"mi sona ala e seme mi wile, taso mi kama sona e ni: wile mi li lon" — I don't know what I want, but I'm coming to know this: my desire exists
"nasin pona li lon ala lon taso wan" — a good way doesn't exist in only one place / there's no single right path
"tenpo ale la, mi tawa. mi tawa ala tawa ma ante. mi tawa insa mi" — always, I move. I don't move to another land. I move inside myself.


sitelen_pona — sitelen pona Hieroglyphs
What it is
The logographic writing system created alongside Toki Pona. One glyph per root word, composable into compound glyphs. Words sit inside a "cartouche" (an oval bracket) when used as proper names.
Core concepts:

Each word has a glyph — often iconic/visual (the glyph for "telo" looks like water; "mun" looks like a crescent moon)
Compound glyphs: one glyph written inside another combines their meanings ("tomo telo" — water-room / bathroom — can be written as the water glyph inside the house glyph)
Proper names in cartouches: the student's adapted name would be written inside an oval

Examples of iconic glyphs to discuss:

"telo" — wave-like lines
"suno" — radiating circle (sun)
"mun" — crescent
"kasi" — plant with stem
"jan" — humanoid figure
"tomo" — house/building shape
"nasin" — a path/road line

Examples of compound glyphs worth exploring:

"ilo toki" (communication device) as a compound
"tomo tawa" (vehicle) as a compound
"jan sona" (wise person/expert) as a compound

Teach:

jan Lina describes 5 glyphs by their shape; student guesses the word
Discuss: why is sitelen pona meaningful in a way English script is not? (The glyph carries the concept, not just the sound)

Practice:

Student describes what each of 8 glyphs might look like, based on the word's meaning
Student designs (in words) their own glyph for 3 words — what would they draw?

Review:

Given descriptions of 5 glyphs, student identifies the words
Student writes their own name in sitelen pona (describes the cartouche contents)


final_exam — Final Mastery Exam
Format:
Part 1 — Written:

Translate 5 complex English sentences into Toki Pona (using pi, preverbs, prepositions, questions)
Write a self-description of 5 sentences minimum using vocabulary from all stages

Part 2 — Conversational:

10-exchange free conversation with jan Lina on a topic from the student's lore
Must include: one yes/no question, one seme question, one anu seme, one pi phrase, one opinion using tawa mi, one preverb

Part 3 — Reflection:

Student writes (in English) what has changed about how they think since learning Toki Pona
What concept was hardest? What surprised them?

Pass criteria: Conversation flows. Errors are self-corrected. The student reaches for Toki Pona before reaching for English.

Boundary notes: what this word does NOT cover, what to use 
instead, and the conceptual reason why. This is the most 
important advanced layer — do not phone it in.

Etymology: where the word actually comes from, source 
language, original meaning.

Mnemonic: a genuine memory hook that creates a real mental 
image or connection. Not a rhyme. Not "pona sounds like 
bonus." Something that actually helps.
